title: Passwords & Account Security
description: Create strong passwords and keep your online accounts safe from hackers.

lessons.title: Why Passwords Matter
lessons.content:
Your password is the lock on your digital life. A weak password is like leaving your front door open.

What are hackers trying to get into?
• Your email account (which can be used to reset ALL your other passwords)
• Your online banking
• Shopping accounts with saved card details
• Social media profiles

😟 What can happen with a weak password?
• Someone could read all your emails
• Money could be taken from your bank account
• Purchases could be made in your name
• Your identity could be stolen
• Your social media could be used to scam your friends

The good news: following a few simple rules makes your accounts dramatically safer. You do not need to be a technology expert to protect yourself well.

🔑 Quick Fact:
The most commonly used passwords in the world are '123456' and 'password'. Millions of people still use these — and millions of accounts are hacked every year as a result.
tips: Think of your email password as the most important one — it can unlock everything else.
A strong password doesn't have to be hard to remember — it just needs to be hard to guess.
Even the strongest password needs protecting — never write it where others can see it.

lessons.title: Creating Strong Passwords
lessons.content:
A strong password has three key qualities:
1️⃣ Long — at least 12 characters
2️⃣ Unpredictable — not your name, birthday, or obvious words
3️⃣ Unique — different for every account

🔑 The 'Three Random Words' Method (recommended by UK National Cyber Security Centre):
Pick three completely random words and combine them, for example:
• PurpleTeapotRocket
• SunflowerBlueBridge
• HappyMonkeyCloud

These are long and unpredictable, yet easy to remember!

💪 Making it even stronger:
Add a number and symbol: PurpleTeapotRocket7!

❌ Avoid using:
• Your name or family members' names
• Your birthday or anniversary
• 'password', '123456', or 'qwerty'
• The name of your pet or street
• Any single word from a dictionary

🔄 Use a DIFFERENT password for EVERY account:
If one account is hacked, criminals try the same password on banking and email. Unique passwords for each account stop this.
tips: The three-random-words technique creates passwords that are both strong and memorable.
Use a password of at least 12 characters — length is the most important factor.
Never use the same password for your email and your bank — these two especially must be different.

lessons.title: Remembering Your Passwords Safely
lessons.content:
With a different password for every account, how do you remember them all? You have a few good options:

📒 Option 1: A Dedicated Password Notebook
Write your passwords in a small notebook kept somewhere safe in your home (not near your computer). This is simple and effective.

What to write:
• Website name
• Your username/email
• Your password

Do NOT label the notebook 'Passwords' — use an inconspicuous name.
Do NOT carry it with you — keep it at home.

💻 Option 2: A Password Manager App
A password manager stores all your passwords securely in one place, protected by one strong 'master password'.

Recommended free password managers:
• Bitwarden — free, highly rated, works on all devices
• Dashlane — free plan available
• Apple Keychain — built into iPhones and Macs
• Google Password Manager — built into Android and Chrome

Benefits: automatically fills in passwords, creates strong passwords for you, and syncs across all your devices.

❌ What NOT to do:
• Do not save passwords in a Word document or email
• Do not use 'sticky notes' on your monitor
• Do not share passwords via text or WhatsApp
tips: A small notebook at home is a perfectly valid and secure way to store passwords — just keep it private.
Password managers are highly recommended by cybersecurity experts — your master password is the only one you need to remember.
If you forget a password, most websites have a simple 'Forgot Password' reset option.

lessons.title: Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)
lessons.content:
Two-factor authentication (2FA) adds a second check when you log in, so even if someone steals your password, they still cannot access your account.

🔐 How it works:
1. You enter your username and password as usual
2. The service sends a one-time code to your mobile phone by text (or generates one in an app)
3. You enter that code to prove it is really you

Because the code changes every time and goes to your phone, a criminal with your password alone cannot get in.

📲 How to turn on 2FA:
• Gmail: Account Settings > Security > 2-Step Verification
• Facebook: Settings > Security and Login > Two-Factor Authentication
• Amazon: Account > Login & Security > Two-Step Verification
• Your bank app may already use 2FA by default

🛡️ Which accounts to protect with 2FA first:
1. Email (most important!)
2. Online banking
3. Social media
4. Shopping accounts with saved cards

💬 Authentication Apps:
For even stronger 2FA, use an app like Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator — these generate codes offline, which are more secure than texts.
tips: Turn on 2FA for your email account first — it protects everything linked to it.
Never share a 2FA code with anyone — not even if they claim to be from your bank or Google.
If you lose your phone, most services have backup 2FA codes you can generate in advance and store safely.

lessons.title: What to Do If You're Hacked
lessons.content:
If you suspect an account has been compromised, act quickly.

🚨 Signs you may have been hacked:
• Emails in your 'Sent' folder that you did not write
• Friends say they received strange messages from you
• You cannot log in to an account you use regularly
• You see purchases you did not make
• You receive 2FA codes you did not request

✅ Immediate Steps:

1. Change your password straight away
If you still have access, go to Settings > Security > Change Password immediately.

2. Change your email password first if that was hacked
Your email is the gateway — secure it first.

3. Check 'Active Sessions' or recent activity
Most services show where you are currently logged in. Log out all unknown devices.

4. Tell your contacts
Let family and friends know your account was hacked so they ignore any strange messages 'from you'.

5. Contact your bank if financial accounts are affected
Call the fraud line on the back of your card immediately.

6. Report to Action Fraud (UK: 0300 123 2040)

🔍 Check if your email has been in a known data breach:
Visit haveibeenpwned.com — a free, safe service run by a respected security researcher.
tips: Act fast — the sooner you change your password after a hack, the less damage is done.
Check haveibeenpwned.com — it's free, safe and tells you if your email appeared in any data breaches.
After a hack, review all accounts you use the same password for and change those too.

quiz.question: Which of these is the STRONGEST example of a password?

options.label: password123
Margaret1952
PurpleTeapotRocket7!
abc

quiz.explanation: Three random unrelated words, combined with a number and symbol, create a password that is both long and unpredictable — the two most important qualities. The others are short or based on personal information hackers could guess.

quiz.question: You use the same password for your email and your online banking. Why is this risky?

options.label: It is not risky — using the same password is more convenient and secure
If one account is hacked, criminals will try the same password on your bank account
You risk forgetting it more easily
Some websites block duplicate passwords

quiz.explanation: Criminals who obtain one of your passwords routinely try it on other popular services — email, banking, shopping. Using unique passwords for each account stops this from spreading.

quiz.question: What is Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)?

options.label: Using two different passwords for the same account
A second check that proves it's you, e.g. a code sent to your phone
Logging in from two different devices
A way to share account access with family safely

quiz.explanation: 2FA adds a second layer of proof that it's really you — usually a one-time code sent to your phone. Even if someone knows your password, they cannot log in without also having your phone.

quiz.question: Someone calls saying they are from Google and asks you to read them a 6-digit code just sent to your phone. What should you do?

options.label: Read the code — they need it to fix your account
Hang up immediately — never share 2FA codes with anyone
Read only the first 3 digits to be cautious
Ask them to email you instead

quiz.explanation: A 2FA code is the key to your account. No legitimate company will ever ask you to read it to them — this is a social engineering attack designed to take over your account.

quiz.question: Where is the SAFEST place to write down your passwords?

options.label: A sticky note on your computer monitor
A Word document on your desktop labelled 'Passwords'
A dedicated notebook kept in a safe private place at home
In a text message to yourself

quiz.explanation: A private notebook stored somewhere secure at home is a perfectly valid option. Avoid digital files or notes near your computer — a physical notebook is only at risk if someone physically finds it.

quiz.question: You notice emails in your Sent folder that you did not write. What should you do first?

options.label: Delete the sent emails and hope nobody noticed
Change your email password immediately
Uninstall the email app
Wait to see if more appear before acting

quiz.explanation: Emails you didn't send appearing in your Sent folder is a clear sign your account has been accessed. Change your password immediately, then check for any other suspicious activity.
